title: Executive Order 10939—To Provide a Guide on Ethical Standards to Government Officials

flags.justification: A code of conduct applicable to officials themselves; it grants no decision-making authority to anyone over anyone else, so there is no concentration to assess.

Sec. 6 requires department and agency heads to 'review or issue internal directives,' but no investigation, complaint process, or consequence mechanism is specified anywhere for an official who actually violates the standards — a genuine gap between stating a norm and providing any means of enforcing it.
Single coherent purpose: ethical standards for senior officials.

Standards are framed in open-textured terms ('not compatible with the full and proper discharge,' 'may result in, or create the appearance of, resulting in') with no defined adjudication process for close cases, compounding the Flag 2 gap.
No incentive concern distinct from the vagueness and enforcement gaps already captured under Flags 2 and 4.
A standing ethics code is evergreen by nature; ethical standards do not require periodic reauthorization the way emergency or operational powers do.
Sec. 7 explicitly states this order does not supersede, alter, or interpret any existing law or regulation.
No external body has any defined role in surfacing or adjudicating violations; the code relies entirely on internal self-policing with no meaningful outside check.

One of the first formalized presidential ethics codes for senior officials, establishing a real normative framework and precedent that later, more robust ethics regulation (eventually including the Ethics in Government Act of 1978) built upon.
No second agency or mandate is implicated.
Sec. 5's carve-outs (bona fide travel reimbursement, charitable/civic participation, merit awards) are narrow, common-sense exceptions, not a broad undermining carve-out.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 4/20 = 20.0%. Historically notable as an early formal presidential ethics code, but the core structural finding is that it states standards without specifying any enforcement mechanism at all — a governance instrument entirely dependent on self-policing. Worth flagging in the notable-findings log.
